title: Key concepts: The origins of Islam
description: Test your knowledge of the origins of Islam!
topic_path: Key concepts: The origins of Islam

assessment_items.question_content:
**How is Muhammad perceived by Muslims?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Muhammad is considered the messenger of God and the Qu'ran is considered to be the Word of God rather than the writings of Muhammad.

choices.content: A divine figure just as Jesus is perceived by Christians
An enlightened man who was the vehicle God used to deliver and spread the Word of God
An enlightened man who wrote the Qur'an based on his ideas

assessment_items.question_content:
**Although not explicity mentioned in the Qur'an, what are considered the 5 pillars of Islam for Sunni Muslims? (85-90% of Muslims)**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Declaring and believing that "There is no god but God" and "Muhammad is God's messenger"
Praying five times a day
Giving a percentage of one's wealth to the poor or needy
Fasting during the holy month of Ramadan
Pilgrimmage to Mecca
Refraining from drinking alcohol
Not eating pork

assessment_items.question_content:
Which of the following best describes the relationship between Islam and Judaism/Christianity?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Jesus is the most referenced figure in the Qur'an. Muslims do not believe him to be divine, but they do believe him to be one of the most significant prophets.
Muslims believe that Judaism and Christianity are fundamentally following the same god and the same traditions as them.  Because of this, Muslims refer to Christians and Jews as "People of the Book."
Muslims do believe that Muhammad's revelations are in the same tradition as the Gospels and the Old Testament of the Bible.

choices.content: Muslims believe that Jesus, Moses and Abraham are significant prophets.  Jesus is, in fact, the most often referenced person in the Quran. They believe that Allah is the same God as in the Old Testament.

Muslims view Judaism and Christianity as completely foreign faiths that have historically  enslaved the Arab people.  They believe that Allah is in a cosmic struggle with Jesus and the god of the Old Testament.
Muslims believe that Allah is the god of the Old Testament, but they reject Jesus as a significant figure (the Qur'an makes no reference to Jesus).

assessment_items.question_content:
How did Muhammad's Quraysh tribe--which controlled Mecca and the pilgrimage site of the Kaaba--initially respond to Muhammad's revelations against idol-worship and the oneness of God?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The Kaaba brought pilgrims from throughout Arabia to Mecca to worship their various deities and idols. This was the source of Mecca's economic power. Muhammad's revelations had the potential to undermine that, so it is believed this is why the Quraysh actively tried to persecute the early Muslims.

choices.content: They welcomed it because it brought more prestige to the Quraysh. The more prestige Muhammad could bring to Mecca, the more likely that worshippers and commerce would come to the city.
They persecuted the early Muslims because the teachings had the potential to undermine the polytheistic idol-worship at the Kaaba.
They were indifferent because they didn't think Muhammad and his followers would matter.

assessment_items.question_content:
What best describes the Arabian Peninsula in the late 6th century (around the time of Muhammad's birth and early life)?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
There was no unified kingdom over Arabia. The Quraysh were a tribe that controlled the city of Mecca.

The Arabian Peninsula had many, fragmented tribes. There was no state structure and people were held in check by tribal law and inter-tribal blood feuds. Some tribes were nomadic  while others were settled in cities.

choices.content: It consisted of many, fragmented tribes. There was no state structure and people were held in check by tribal law and inter-tribal blood feuds. Some tribes were nomadic  while others were settled in cities.
It was fragmented, but because of how sparsely populated it was, it was generally peaceful compared to life under a unified kingdom.
It was unified under the Quraysh Dynasty with its capital at Mecca. This kingdom has tight control over the lives of the people of the Arabian Peninsula.

assessment_items.question_content:
What best describes the state of the Muslim community at the time of Muhammad's death in 632?


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: At the time of Muhammad's death, the Muslims had conquered Mecca and much of the Arabian Peninsula. The Quraysh had converted to Islam.

choices.content: They had conquered Mecca and much of the Arabian Peninsula.
They were actively persecuted and were mainly confined to the area around Medina.
They had splintered into to rival factions. One controlling Eastern Arabia, the other controlling the West.

assessment_items.question_content:
What best describes Muhammad's role in the early Muslim community?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Not only was Muhammad a spiritual figure, but he lead the early Muslims both politically and militarily.

choices.content: He was a purely spiritual figure who advised the local tribal rulers.
He was a spiritual, political, and military figure.
He was a spiritual and political figure, but refused to fight.

assessment_items.question_content:
What is the significance of Medina in the origin of Islam?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Medina was the city that the early Muslims settled in to escape persecution from the Quraysh in Mecca.  It was their base in the years of conflict with the Quraysh.

choices.content: It was the city that the early Muslims settled in to escape persecution from the Quraysh in Mecca.  It was their base in the years of conflict with the Quraysh.
It was the home of Muhammad and a pilgrimage site for the tribes of Arabia. It was initially controlled by the Quraysh, but eventually taken over by the Muslims.
It was the home of a rival religious group. The early Muslims had multiple battles with the inhabitants of Medina.
